target_question: Why does changing a list inside a function affect the original?
description: How Python passes objects by reference to their identity, not by copying values
initial_misconception: Python is either fully pass-by-value or pass-by-reference, and I can't tell which.
initial_student_statement: I passed a list to a function and modified it inside, and the original list changed too! But when I reassigned an integer inside a function, the original didn't change. This is so inconsistent!
correct_understanding: Python uses 'pass by object reference' (pass by assignment). The function receives a reference to the same object. Mutating the object (like list.append) changes the original because it's the same object. Reassigning the variable (x = new_value) only changes the local name binding, not the original object.
good_question_templates: When you pass a list to a function, what exactly is being passed — the list itself or something else?
What's the difference between modifying a list's contents and replacing a variable's value entirely?
If I give you a piece of paper with an address on it, can you repaint the house? Can you change what address is on the paper?
What happens when you do lst.append(x) versus lst = [x]?
Can you check if the list inside the function is literally the same object as the one outside?

confused: I don't know, I thought Python just copies things when you pass them to functions?
If it's pass by reference, why doesn't reassigning x inside the function change x outside?
uncertain: So... the function gets access to the same list, not a copy? Like a shared pointer?
Hmm, so modifying the list's contents is different from pointing the variable at a new list?
starting_to_see: Oh! So when I do lst.append(), I'm changing the actual list object. But when I do lst = [new], I'm just making the local variable point somewhere else?
It's like the function has a sticky note with the address of the list. It can change what's inside the house, but scribbling a new address on the sticky note doesn't move the house!
almost_there: So Python passes the reference to the object. Mutating operations act on the original, but assignment just rebinds the local name. That's why integers seem pass-by-value — you can't mutate them, only reassign!
I see — it's not pass-by-value OR pass-by-reference in the C sense. It's more like pass-by-object-reference. The reference is passed by value.
understood: I get it! Python passes object references by value. The function gets a copy of the reference pointing to the same object. Mutating the object (append, index assignment) changes the original. Reassigning the variable just rebinds the local name. Immutable objects 'seem' pass-by-value because you can't mutate them — only reassign.

Now I understand the whole picture — it's pass by object reference. The key is distinguishing mutation (changes the object) from rebinding (changes what the name points to). That's why list.append affects the caller but x = new_value doesn't.
